title: Your first for loop
instruction:
Imagine printing the names of 100 students. Writing 100 `print()` calls is insane.

A `for` loop does repetition for you:

```python
for item in collection:
    # runs once per item
```

Run this. Python loops through the list and runs the block once per name.
successMessage: Five names, five iterations. The loop handled all five with four lines of code. Scale that to a million students — still four lines.

title: range() — loop a number of times
instruction:
`range(n)` produces the numbers 0, 1, 2, ... n-1.

`range(3)` → 0, 1, 2
`range(1, 6)` → 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
`range(0, 10, 2)` → 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 (step of 2)

Print the numbers 1 to 10 using `range()`.

codeChecks.message: Use range(1, 11) to get 1 through 10.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: range(1, 11) gives you 1 to 10. The stop value is always exclusive — range(1, 11) stops before 11. Use this whenever you need a counter.

title: Loop over a string
instruction:
Strings are sequences too — you can loop over each character:

`for char in "hello":` → visits `h`, `e`, `l`, `l`, `o`

Write a loop that prints each letter of your name on its own line. Then count how many characters it has by incrementing a counter.
hint:
count = 0
for char in name:
    print(char)
    count += 1
print(f"Total: {count}")
starter:
name = "Python"
# TODO: loop over name and print each character
# also count and print total characters at the end

codeChecks.message: Loop over name: for char in name:
Print each character.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Strings are just sequences of characters. Every for loop that works on a list also works on a string.

title: enumerate() — index and value together
instruction:
Sometimes you need the position (index) as well as the value while looping.

`enumerate()` gives you both:

```python
for index, value in enumerate(collection):
```

Rewrite the loop below using `enumerate()` to print the index alongside each student's name.
starter:
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol"]

# TODO: use enumerate() to print:
# 1. Alice
# 2. Bob
# 3. Carol
for student in students:
    print(student)

codeChecks.message: Use: for i, student in enumerate(students):
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: enumerate returns (index, value) pairs. Note: by default it starts at 0 — use enumerate(students, 1) to start numbering at 1.

title: while — loop until a condition is False
instruction:
`while` loops run as long as a condition is `True`:

```python
while condition:
    # runs as long as condition is True
```

Use `while` when you do not know in advance how many times to loop.

Add the missing update (`count += 1`) so the loop does not run forever.
starter:
count = 1

while count <= 5:
    print(f"Count: {count}")
    # TODO: increment count or this loops forever!

codeChecks.message: Increment count: count += 1
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Without count += 1, while True loops forever — an infinite loop. Always make sure your while loop condition will eventually become False.

title: break — exit the loop early
instruction:
`break` immediately exits the loop — even if there are items left to process.

This is perfect for searches: once you find what you are looking for, stop.

Add a `break` so the search stops as soon as it finds `"Carol"`.
starter:
students = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol", "David", "Eve"]

for student in students:
    print(f"Checking: {student}")
    # TODO: if student is Carol, print found message and break

codeChecks.message: Use break to exit the loop early.
Check for Carol and break.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: David and Eve were never checked. break saved unnecessary work. In a list of a million users, finding at position 3 and breaking saves 999,997 iterations.

title: continue — skip to the next iteration
instruction:
`continue` skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next one.

Use it to skip items that do not meet a condition — without breaking out of the loop.

Add a `continue` to skip negative numbers and only sum the positive ones.
starter:
numbers = [5, -3, 8, -1, 4, -7, 2]
total = 0

for num in numbers:
    # TODO: skip negatives using continue
    total += num

print(f"Sum of positives: {total}")

codeChecks.message: Use continue to skip negative numbers.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: 5 + 8 + 4 + 2 = 19. The negatives were skipped. continue says 'done with this one, move on' — break says 'done with the whole loop'.

title: zip() — loop two lists in parallel
instruction:
`zip()` lets you loop over two (or more) lists simultaneously:

```python
for a, b in zip(list1, list2):
```

Use zip to pair names with scores and print each pairing.
hint: for name, score in zip(names, scores):
starter:
names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Carol"]
scores = [92, 78, 95]

# TODO: use zip to print each name with their score
# Expected:
# Alice: 92
# Bob: 78
# Carol: 95

codeChecks.message: Use: for name, score in zip(names, scores):
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: zip walks both lists in lockstep. When one list runs out, zip stops. Super clean — no indexing, no enumerate, just natural pairing.

title: Nested loops
instruction:
A loop inside a loop — the inner loop runs fully for every iteration of the outer.

Be careful: 10 × 10 = 100 iterations. 1000 × 1000 = 1,000,000.

Complete the multiplication table using nested loops.
starter:
# TODO: print a 3x3 multiplication table using nested for loops
# 1 x 1 = 1
# 1 x 2 = 2
# ...
# 3 x 3 = 9

codeChecks.message: Use nested for loops.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Nested loops. 9 lines of output from 5 lines of code. Outer loop runs 3 times, inner loop runs 3 times per outer iteration: 3 × 3 = 9 total.

title: List comprehension — loop in one line
instruction:
List comprehension creates a new list by looping in a single expression:

```python
[expression for item in collection]
```

`[x * 2 for x in numbers]` — doubles every number in the list.

Rewrite the four-line loop as a list comprehension.
starter:
# Standard loop:
result = []
for x in range(1, 6):
    result.append(x ** 2)
print(result)

# TODO: rewrite as a list comprehension
squares = []  # replace this
print(squares)

codeChecks.message: Use list comprehension: [x**2 for x in range(1, 6)]
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: One line does what four lines did. List comprehensions are one of Python's best features — fast, readable, and Pythonic.

title: Build a FizzBuzz
instruction:
Classic programming challenge:

- Print numbers 1 to 20
- If divisible by 3: print `Fizz`
- If divisible by 5: print `Buzz`
- If divisible by both: print `FizzBuzz`
- Otherwise: print the number

Use `%` (modulo) — `n % 3 == 0` means n is divisible by 3.
hint: Check FizzBuzz first (both), then Fizz, then Buzz, then the number.

codeChecks.message: Use a for loop with range.
Check divisibility by 3: n % 3 == 0
Check divisibility by 5: n % 5 == 0
Handle the FizzBuzz case (divisible by both).
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: FizzBuzz is done. You have passed the first question of almost every programming interview. Chapter 7 complete.
